item.title: What is a token
item.description: AI is meant to seem like magic. But there's no such thing as magic. It's all illusion. So, allow me to spoil that illusion for you.

item.title: We Automated Everything Except Knowing What's Going On
item.description: The personal site of whom Kenneth has designed and built impactful systems that power global-scale operations, improve security, and drive efficiency. From leading infrastructure transformations at Cloudflare to optimizing AI-driven platforms at Balto, his career reflects a deep understanding of how technology shapes industries. He thrives at the intersection of engineering, business, and innovation, advising startups, conducting technical due diligence for venture capital firms, and even exploring creative storytelling through film production. Kenneth believes engineering should not only solve problems but also inspire new possibilities—pushing the boundaries of what’s possible while ensuring systems remain practical, resilient, and human-centered

item.title: The Shady World of IP Leasing
item.description: The IPv4

item.title: Risk, in Perspective
item.description: In my cushy 2026 tech bubble in San Francisco, risk-taking is a personality trait. The failure case, for most in my circles, is going back to a six-figure salary. My grandparents would not have recognized this as risk at all.

item.title: The Social Media Discoverability Problem
item.description: As a gay kid from an isolated suburb, algorithmic feeds showed me the interests that changed my life. How do we build a healthy form of social media that provides this for future generations?

item.title: Designing the Perfect ID: Marrying UUIDv7, Stripe Prefixes, and ULID · Technical Blog · Alcazar Security
item.description: Designing the Perfect ID: Marrying UUIDv7, Stripe Prefixes, and ULID

item.title: What if code wasn't a text document?
Fine-Tuning Qwen3 Embeddings for product category classification on the Large-Scale Product Corpus
item.description: Language-models such as GPT, Llama, DeepSeek, Qwen trained with a filtered slice of Common Crawl. For e-commerce work, though, we can start…

item.title: Voice Can Make Coding Agents Better (In Some Cases!) — Nima Sadri
item.description: Building ata, an open-source AI research agent

item.title: ADAPT: the server to bring back the (Social) Web · Raphael Lullis
I built a self-hosted RSS system that actually keeps me informed — Jordan Krueger
item.description: Email newsletters were drowning me. Algorithm feeds were wasting my time. So I used Claude Code to build a self-hosted RSS setup with Miniflux, NetNewsWire, and an AI filter that only shows me stuff worth reading.

item.title: It’s Always DNS — Until You’re Stuck and Can’t Fix It
item.description: DNS fails in specific, predictable ways: UDP truncation, TTL propagation lag, and the distributed deadlock that occurs when your recovery tools depend on the thing that is broken.

item.title: Building My Own Canva Over a Weekend
item.description: I was manually turning generated children's books into Instagram carousels, hit the limit of “doing things that don't scale,” and built a focused internal editor to make the workflow deterministic and fast.

item.title: The Data Doesn't Speak
item.description: Your formation shapes what the data can tell you.

item.title: What's new in Linux kernel... for PostgreSQL · Erthalion's blog
Two months as a vibe coder - Pinch of Intelligence
item.description: The last two months my work completely changed. My job changed from thinking long and hard about problems and coming up with the best few code to solve this, to full-on talking in English to my computer begging it to produce better code. In this blog I am sharing my observations and feelings in this brave new world. Productivity First…Read more →

item.title: Working On Things That Suck
item.description:
Have you ever been working on a project and realise what you're working on sucks? Like it just isn't good enough for anyone to find it useful.
I've recen...

item.title: Breaking Up with TurboTax: How I Used Claude to File My Taxes for Free
item.description: A journey from paid tax software to AI-assisted free filing using Claude and IRS Free File Fillable Forms.

item.title: Stop asking junior engineers to struggle harder
item.description: Four pieces landed in the past two weeks that circle the same problem from different altitudes.

item.title: mpv Is the MVP of Video and Image Viewing — Nick Janetakis
item.description: Since I switched to native Linux, I really missed IrfanView for image viewing but mpv is filling that gap quite nicely.

item.title: Iran War Cost Tracker — Live Estimate of U.S. Taxpayer Spending
item.description: Track the cost of the Iran war in real time. Live estimate of U.S. taxpayer spending on Operation Epic Fury — updated every second.

item.title: Apple introduces the new MacBook Air with M5
item.description: Apple today announced the new MacBook Air with M5, bringing exceptional performance and expanded AI capabilities to the world’s most popular laptop.

item.title: Nobody Gets Promoted for Simplicity
item.description: We reward complexity and ignore simplicity. In interviews, design reviews, and promotions. Here’s how to fix it.

item.title: I'm struggling to think of any online services for which I'd be willing to verify my identity or age
item.description: Identity verification and age verification is an increasinly common policy conversation at the moment, in numerous countries.

item.description: Apple announced the latest 14- and 16-inch MacBook Pro with the all-new M5 Pro and M5 Max.

item.title: XKCD Dependency
item.description: A web editor for p5.js, a JavaScript library with the goal of making coding accessible to artists, designers, educators, and beginners.

item.title: Open source package repositories face sustainability crisis
item.description: Opinion: Careless big-time users are treating FOSS repos like content delivery networks

item.title: Electric Vehicles
Giving LLMs a personality is just good engineering
item.description: AI skeptics often argue that current AI systems shouldn’t be so human-like. The idea - most recently expressed in this opinion piece by Nathan Beacom - is that language models should explicitly be tools, like calculators or search engines. Although they can pretend to be people, they shouldn’t, because it encourages users to overestimate AI capabilities and (at worst) slip into AI psychosis. Here’s a representative paragraph from the piece:

item.title: Welcome (back) to Macintosh
item.description: For at least 10 years, every Time Machine set up I have been in charge of, or tasked with maintaining for someone else, has eventually run into an issue where it stops backing up successfully. The only solution has been to start over, to not inherit backup state and to manually delete the old backup. My experience while looking up this solution repeatedly has been that this is not uncommon, and definitely is not just me holding it wrong; indeed the steps are marked as recommended solutions on Apple's own forums.
